Threat.value: Having strong physical reactions when something reminded you of the stressful experience
Taking too many risks or doing things that could cause you harm
Irritable behavior, angry outbursts, or acting aggressively

Memory.value: When I remember the frightening event I feel like it is happening right now.
My memories of the frightening event are like a film that plays over and over.
I remember the frightening event as a few moments, and each moment is a picture in my mind.

Negative Appraisals.value: I can't rely on other people
I can't trust that I will do the right thing

vignette:
The patient is a 76-year-old non-binary person from Oceania who is widowed and presents with severe, persistent post-traumatic stress symptoms following an interpersonal trauma. Their distress is reflected in a very high PCL-5 score of 79. In day-to-day life, they are often unsettled by uncertainty, crowded places, and old photographs, and these reminders intrude into ordinary routines and narrow where they feel able to go. They describe strong physical reactions when something calls the event to mind, alongside irritability, angry outbursts, and episodes of taking unnecessary risks or doing things that could put them in harm’s way.

Their memories of the frightening event are fragmented and overpowering: they describe it as if it is happening again in the present, as a film that repeats over and over, and as a series of still images that arrive in sharp, disconnected snapshots. These memory experiences are central to the way they remain stuck, repeatedly drawing them back into distress and making familiar settings feel unsafe. They avoid exercise, keep checking mirrors, and steer clear of police, and these patterns have gradually restricted their independence and confidence.

Alongside this, the patient holds deeply pessimistic views of themselves and other people. They often say they cannot rely on other people, cannot trust themselves to do the right thing, and cannot rely on themselves. These beliefs sit heavily in the background and are accompanied by a persistent sense of danger and bodily alarm. When the patient encounters reminders of the trauma, the sense of threat intensifies quickly, and their physical reactions become especially pronounced.

Over time, the combination of intrusive memory, heightened threat, and avoidance has taken a clear toll on daily functioning and relationships. The patient’s world has become increasingly circumscribed, with crowding, uncertainty, and visual reminders such as old photos all carrying emotional weight. Their vigilance, mirror checking, and avoidance of exercise and police shape much of their routine, and their life now feels organized around managing the next reminder rather than moving freely through it.

vignette_attempts.vignette:
The patient is a 76-year-old non-binary person from Oceania who is widowed and presents with severe, persistent post-traumatic stress symptoms following an interpersonal trauma. Their distress is reflected in a very high PCL-5 score of 79. In day-to-day life, they are often unsettled by uncertainty, crowded places, and old photographs, with these reminders intruding into ordinary routines and narrowing where they feel able to go. They describe strong physical reactions when something calls the event to mind, alongside irritability, angry outbursts, and episodes of taking unnecessary risks or doing things that could put them in harm’s way.

Their memories of the frightening event are fragmented and overpowering: they describe it as if it is happening again in the present, as a film that repeats over and over, and as a series of still images that arrive in sharp, disconnected snapshots. These memory experiences are central to the way they remain stuck, repeatedly drawing them back into distress and making familiar settings feel unsafe. They avoid exercise, keep checking mirrors, and steer clear of police, and these patterns have gradually restricted their independence and confidence.

Alongside this, the patient holds deeply pessimistic views of themselves and other people. They often say they cannot rely on other people, cannot trust themselves to do the right thing, and cannot rely on themselves. These beliefs sit heavily in the background and are accompanied by a persistent sense of danger and bodily alarm. When the patient encounters reminders of the trauma, the sense of threat intensifies quickly, and their physical reactions become especially pronounced.

Over time, the combination of intrusive memory, heightened threat, and avoidance has taken a clear toll on daily functioning and relationships. The patient’s world has become increasingly circumscribed, with crowding, uncertainty, and visual reminders such as old photos all carrying emotional weight. Their vigilance, mirror checking, and avoidance of exercise and police appear to offer brief relief, but they also keep the trauma highly salient and maintain a life organized around staying safe.

violations.explanation: The vignette says the patient's vigilance, mirror checking, and avoidance 'keep the trauma highly salient,' which is explicit causal language from maladaptive strategies to memory-like trauma salience.
violations.quote: their vigilance, mirror checking, and avoidance of exercise and police appear to offer brief relief, but they also keep the trauma highly salient
